skill.name: network-101
skill.description: Guides users through building isolated lab services for HTTP, HTTPS, SNMP, and SMB testing, with enumeration and troubleshooting references.
skill.summary: Configure and test common network services in controlled penetration testing labs.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many external command and network indicators. Review confirmed that most Ruby backtick alerts are Markdown code-fence false positives, but the skill still contains high-risk dual-use lab instructions for privileged service changes, insecure SNMP and SMB exposure, and network enumeration. No evidence of prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, so this is high risk rather than blocked.

high_findings.title: Insecure SNMP Configuration and Enumeration
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to configure public and private SNMP community strings, use SNMP v1, enumerate system information, list running processes, and brute force community strings. This is useful in an isolated lab but can expose sensitive host data or support unauthorized reconnaissance if used on real networks.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands explicitly configure weak community strings and run enumeration tools. The lab context is stated, but the technical risk is direct and reproducible.

high_findings.title: Overly Permissive SMB Share Exposure
high_findings.description: The skill shows world-writable SMB directories, anonymous access, guest access, and SMB enumeration with vulnerability scripts. These settings are dangerous outside a controlled lab because they can expose files and weaken host security.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains chmod 777, guest access, anonymous SMB connection examples, and SMB vulnerability scanning. These are concrete risky configurations and commands.

high_findings.title: Privileged Service and Firewall Changes
high_findings.description: The guide repeatedly uses sudo to install services, enable daemons, edit system configuration, write web files, change firewall rules, and generate certificate files. This can materially alter a host and expose services if a user applies it outside an isolated lab.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The privileged commands are explicit and numerous. The intent appears educational, but the commands can change real systems and open network services.

medium_findings.title: Network Scanning and Vulnerability Checks
medium_findings.description: The skill includes port checks, SNMP walks, SMB enumeration, brute forcing references, and vulnerability script scanning against target hosts. These commands are legitimate in a lab but are dual-use and require clear authorization boundaries.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network tooling is clearly present and usable for reconnaissance. The stated lab purpose lowers malicious confidence but not operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Credential Collection Context in Web Lab
medium_findings.description: The examples create a login form with a password field and include a section titled around parsing logs for credentials. No credential exfiltration code was found, but this context needs guardrails to avoid collecting real secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The password form and credential-oriented log review are visible, but the file does not show storage or exfiltration of submitted credentials.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misread as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick execution hits, but the reviewed locations are Markdown fenced command examples, not executable Ruby code. These are false positives for code execution, although some enclosed commands remain risky for other reasons.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence lines show Markdown fences and shell examples inside SKILL.md. There is no Ruby source file or executable backtick expression.

dangerous_patterns.title: World-Writable Service Directories
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses chmod 777 for SMB share directories. This pattern can allow broad write access and should be constrained to disposable lab systems.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The chmod 777 commands are explicit at both locations. The resulting permissions are broadly writable by design.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak SNMP Defaults
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide uses public and private community strings with SNMP v1 examples. These defaults are widely known and expose management data in cleartext.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact weak community strings and SNMP v1 flags are present. The file also demonstrates enumeration using those values.

dangerous_patterns.title: Service Persistence Enablement
dangerous_patterns.description: The Apache setup enables a network service at boot. This is expected for administration but increases exposure if copied to a non-lab host.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The systemctl enable command is explicit. It is not malicious by itself, but it creates persistent service exposure.

content.value_statement: Network service labs are hard to assemble consistently and safely. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured guidance for isolated HTTP, HTTPS, SNMP, and SMB practice environments.
actual_capabilities: Outlines HTTP and HTTPS service setup for Windows IIS and Linux Apache labs.
Provides SNMP setup and enumeration workflows for controlled practice targets.
Describes SMB share creation and enumeration commands for lab systems.
Lists common ports, verification commands, and troubleshooting checks.
Includes log review examples for Apache and IIS service analysis.
limitations: The instructions are unsafe for production systems without hardening changes.
The skill does not enforce authorization or network isolation by itself.
Examples use weak SNMP and SMB settings for training purposes.
It does not replace vendor documentation or a secure deployment baseline.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Training Lab
use_cases.description: Create repeatable HTTP, HTTPS, SNMP, and SMB targets for a private classroom environment.

use_cases.title: Practice Service Enumeration
use_cases.description: Review which tools and commands identify exposed services on an authorized lab host.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot Lab Connectivity
use_cases.description: Check ports, firewall rules, service status, and logs when a training target does not respond.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Safe Lab
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan an isolated network lab for HTTP, HTTPS, SNMP, and SMB practice. Include safety boundaries and prerequisites.

prompt_templates.title: Configure One Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through configuring Apache HTTP in a disposable lab VM. Include checks for service status and firewall access.

prompt_templates.title: Run Authorized Enumeration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an authorized enumeration checklist for my lab target at a private IP address. Cover HTTP, HTTPS, SNMP, and SMB.

prompt_templates.title: Review Lab Risk
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this planned network lab configuration for unsafe defaults. Recommend safer alternatives after the exercise is complete.

output_examples.input: I need a small HTTP and HTTPS lab for students.
output: A prerequisite checklist for the host, firewall, and test machine.
A step sequence for installing and verifying the web service.
A safety note to keep the lab isolated and remove the service after use.

output_examples.input: My SMB lab share is not visible from Kali.
output: A connectivity checklist covering port 445, service state, and firewall rules.
Permission checks for share access and local filesystem access.
Log locations to review for authentication or connection errors.

output_examples.input: Help me document an SNMP enumeration exercise.
output: A lab-only scope statement and target authorization note.
A table of expected SNMP observations and commands used.
A cleanup section recommending removal of weak communities.

best_practices: Use only disposable systems or private lab networks that you own or administer.
Replace weak example settings with hardened values after each exercise.
Document authorization, target IPs, changes made, and cleanup actions.
anti_patterns: Do not run enumeration commands against networks without written authorization.
Do not copy weak SNMP or anonymous SMB settings into production environments.
Do not expose lab services to the public internet.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for production use?
faq.answer: No. It is intended for isolated training labs and includes intentionally weak examples.

faq.question: Can it configure services automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and command examples, but users execute changes themselves.

faq.question: Does it require administrator access?
faq.answer: Yes. Many setup steps require administrative privileges on the lab host.

faq.question: Which services does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers HTTP, HTTPS, SNMP, SMB, common ports, verification checks, and basic logs.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is marked for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatibility.

faq.question: What should I clean up after testing?
faq.answer: Remove weak communities, anonymous shares, broad permissions, open firewall rules, and unneeded services.
